chunks.document: C L O U D C O M P U T I N G · D E V O P S $ docker run fundamentals Docker Fundamentals & Containerization Lec by Imran Sayyed2.5 hour sessionFor first-time learners S E S S I O N R O A D M A P Six steps from problem to container 01 Foundations Why deployment breaks 02 VMs vs Containers Two ways to isolate 03 Docker Architecture Client, engine, registry 04 Images & Containers Pull, run, manage 05 Dockerfile Build your own image 06 Cloud & Wrap-up Real-world & review T H E D E P L O Y M E N T P R O B L E M “But it works on my machine” The same code behaves differently across environments because the surroundings are never identical. ✕Different OS versions ✕Missing dependencies ✕Mismatched config Developer runs fine ✓ → Tester crashes ? → Production breaks ✕ W H Y C O N T A I N E R S E X I S T Package the app with everything it needs consistent Same everywhere Runs identically on a laptop, a server, or the cloud. portable Ship as one unit Code, runtime, libraries, and config travel toget

T Package the app with everything it needs consistent Same everywhere Runs identically on a laptop, a server, or the cloud. portable Ship as one unit Code, runtime, libraries, and config travel together. efficient Light & fast Starts in seconds and shares the host kernel. T R A D I T I O N A L D E P L O Y M E N T One app, one server Apps were installed straight onto physical machines, tied to that server’s exact setup. •Slow to provision new servers •Hard to scale or move •Wasted, idle capacity Application ↓ Operating System ↓ Physical Hardware V I R T U A L M A C H I N E A R C H I T E C T U R E Each VM carries its own full OS App A Guest OS App B Guest OS App C Guest OS ↑ Hypervisor ↑ Host Operating System Physical Hardware P R O B L E M S W I T H V I R T U A L M A C H I N E S Powerful, but heavy 01 Large in size Each VM bundles a full OS — often many gigabytes. 02 Slow to boot Starting a guest OS takes minutes, not seconds. 03 Resource overhead Every VM reserves its own CPU and memo

but heavy 01 Large in size Each VM bundles a full OS — often many gigabytes. 02 Slow to boot Starting a guest OS takes minutes, not seconds. 03 Resource overhead Every VM reserves its own CPU and memory. 04 Fewer per host A single server runs only a handful of VMs. I N T R O D U C T I O N T O C O N T A I N E R S Isolation without a full OS A container is a lightweight, isolated package that holds an app and its dependencies — sharing the host’s kernel instead of shipping a whole operating system. ✓Starts in seconds ✓Megabytes, not gigabytes ✓Many per host CONTAINER Your Application Libraries Dependencies C O N T A I N E R A R C H I T E C T U R E One shared kernel, many containers App A + libs App B + libs App C + libs ↑ Docker Engine ↑ Host Operating System Physical Hardware V M S V S C O N T A I N E R S Same goal, very different weight Virtual Machines IsolationFull OS per VM SizeGigabytes StartupMinutes OverheadHigh Per hostFew Containers IsolationShared kernel SizeMegabytes StartupS

N T A I N E R S Same goal, very different weight Virtual Machines IsolationFull OS per VM SizeGigabytes StartupMinutes OverheadHigh Per hostFew Containers IsolationShared kernel SizeMegabytes StartupSeconds OverheadLow Per hostMany I N T R O D U C I N G D O C K E R The standard way to run containers Docker is an open platform that builds, ships, and runs applications inside containers — turning containerization into a simple, repeatable workflow. →Build images from a recipe →Ship them through a registry →Run anywhere Docker is installed DOCKER Build · Ship · Run any environment T H E D O C K E R E C O S Y S T E M The moving parts you will use docker CLI Docker Client The commands you type to control Docker. dockerd Docker Engine The daemon that builds and runs containers. images Images & Containers Templates and their running instances. registry Docker Hub The cloud registry that stores images. D O C K E R A R C H I T E C T U R E Client, daemon, and registry CLIENT Docker Client docker

ontainers Templates and their running instances. registry Docker Hub The cloud registry that stores images. D O C K E R A R C H I T E C T U R E Client, daemon, and registry CLIENT Docker Client docker run ... REST API → DAEMON Docker Engine imagescontainers pull / push → REGISTRY Docker Hub stored images W H A T I S A N I M A G E ? A read-only template An image is a packaged blueprint of your app — built in stacked layers and frozen so every container starts from the exact same state. ✓Read-only and reusable ✓Built from layers ✓Shared via a registry App layer Dependencies Runtime Base OS layer stacked, read-only layers I M A G E L I F E C Y C L E From recipe to running app RECIPE Dockerfile instructions docker build → TEMPLATE Image read-only docker run → INSTANCE Container running W H A T I S A C O N T A I N E R ? A running instance of an image If an image is the recipe, a container is the finished dish — a live, isolated process created from the image with its own writable layer on t

I S A C O N T A I N E R ? A running instance of an image If an image is the recipe, a container is the finished dish — a live, isolated process created from the image with its own writable layer on top. →Created from an image →Isolated and writable →Many from one image IMAGE nginx → web-1 web-2 web-3 C O N T A I N E R L I F E C Y C L E Create, run, stop, remove create Created from image → start Running doing work → stop Stopped paused → rm Removed gone W H A T I S D O C K E R H U B ? The public library of images Docker Hub is a cloud registry where official and community images are published, searched, and pulled. →Official, trusted images →Versioned with tags →Free to pull and use hub.docker.comQ search images nginx OFFICIAL · 1B+ pulls node OFFICIAL · 1B+ pulls postgres OFFICIAL · 1B+ pulls P U L L I N G I M A G E S Download an image from the registry docker pull fetches an image to your machine, layer by layer. →Defaults to the latest tag →Cached layers are reused bash $ docker pul

ulls P U L L I N G I M A G E S Download an image from the registry docker pull fetches an image to your machine, layer by layer. →Defaults to the latest tag →Cached layers are reused bash $ docker pull nginx Using default tag: latest latest: Pulling from library/nginx a2abf6c4d29d: Pull complete 9b48060f404d: Pull complete Status: Downloaded newer image for nginx:latest R U N N I N G C O N T A I N E R S Turn an image into a live container docker run creates and starts a container from an image in one step. →Pulls the image if missing →-druns in the background bash $ docker run -d -p 8080:80 nginx 3f9a1c7b2e64d8a0c1... # container started in background $ docker ps CONTAINER ID IMAGE STATUS 3f9a1c7b2e64 nginx Up 3 seconds H E L L O W O R L D E X A M P L E Your first container hello-world is a tiny image that proves your Docker install works end to end. →Pulls the image →Runs and prints a message →Exits immediately bash $ docker run hello-world Unable to find image locally latest: Pulling

a tiny image that proves your Docker install works end to end. →Pulls the image →Runs and prints a message →Exits immediately bash $ docker run hello-world Unable to find image locally latest: Pulling from hello-world Hello from Docker! This message shows your install is working correctly. N G I N X E X A M P L E Run a real web server bash $ docker run -d \ -p 8080:80 nginx 7c2b9f1a... # nginx now serving on # host port 8080 → localhost:8080 Welcome to nginx! If you see this page, the nginx web server is working. P O R T M A P P I N G Connect the outside to the inside -p 8080:80 # host : container USER Browser :8080 → DOCKER HOST Port 8080 maps to → → CONTAINER Port 80 nginx C O N T A I N E R N E T W O R K I N G B A S I C S How containers talk bridge Default network Containers on one host share a private bridge. name Service discovery Containers reach each other by container name. -p Published ports Map a port to expose a container to the outside. V O L U M E S I N T R O D U C T I O N

share a private bridge. name Service discovery Containers reach each other by container name. -p Published ports Map a port to expose a container to the outside. V O L U M E S I N T R O D U C T I O N Storage that outlives the container By default, anything written inside a container disappears when it is removed. A volume is managed storage that lives outside the container’s writable layer. ✓Survives restarts and removal ✓Shareable between containers ✓Managed by Docker Container ↓ writes to Volume persistent storage W H Y V O L U M E S M A T T E R Containers are disposable, data is not Without a volume ✕Data lives in the container ✕Removed with the container ✕Nothing to recover With a volume ✓Data stored outside ✓Survives container removal ✓Reattach to a new container V O L U M E A R C H I T E C T U R E One volume, mounted into a container -v mydata:/var/lib/data CONTAINER App writes to /var/lib/data mount → VOLUME mydata managed by Docker on → DISK Host Storage persists D O C K E R C

T E C T U R E One volume, mounted into a container -v mydata:/var/lib/data CONTAINER App writes to /var/lib/data mount → VOLUME mydata managed by Docker on → DISK Host Storage persists D O C K E R C O M M A N D S O V E R V I E W The everyday toolkit docker pull Download an image docker run Start a container docker ps List containers docker stop Stop a container docker rm Remove a container docker images List local images C O M M A N D · D O C K E R P U L L Get an image locally docker pull <image.:<tag> →Downloads from Docker Hub →Tag defaults tolatest →Reuses cached layers $ docker pull python:3.12 3.12: Pulling from library/python f1c2e9a7...: Pull complete Downloaded python:3.12 C O M M A N D · D O C K E R R U N Create and start a container docker run [opts] <image> -drun in the background -ppublish a port .-namename the container $ docker run -d \ .-name web \ -p 8080:80 nginx a1b2c3d4... # running as "web" C O M M A N D · D O C K E R P S See what is running docker ps [-a] →Lists r

und -ppublish a port .-namename the container $ docker run -d \ .-name web \ -p 8080:80 nginx a1b2c3d4... # running as "web" C O M M A N D · D O C K E R P S See what is running docker ps [-a] →Lists running containers -ashows stopped ones too →Shows IDs, status, ports $ docker ps CONTAINER ID IMAGE STATUS PORTS a1b2c3d4e5f6 nginx Up 2 minutes 0.0.0.0:8080.>80 9f8e7d6c5b4a redis Up 5 minutes 6379/tcp C O M M A N D · D O C K E R S T O P Gracefully halt a container docker stop <name|id> →Sends a stop signal →Lets the app shut down cleanly →Container can be restarted $ docker stop web web $ docker ps CONTAINER ID IMAGE STATUS # web no longer listed C O M M A N D · D O C K E R R M Remove a container docker rm <name|id> !Stop it first -fforce-remove a running one !Data without a volume is lost $ docker stop web $ docker rm web web # container removed D O C K E R F I L E I N T R O D U C T I O N A recipe for your image A Dockerfile is a plain-text file with step-by- step instructions. Docker r

docker stop web $ docker rm web web # container removed D O C K E R F I L E I N T R O D U C T I O N A recipe for your image A Dockerfile is a plain-text file with step-by- step instructions. Docker reads it top to bottom and builds an image you can run anywhere. →Repeatable and version-controlled →Each line is a build step →Steps are cached as layers # Dockerfile FROM python:3.12 WORKDIR /app COPY . . RUN pip install -r reqs.txt CMD ["python", "app.py"] D O C K E R F I L E S T R U C T U R E Read it like a story FROM Choose a base image to start from WORKDIR Set the working directory inside the image COPY Add your application files RUN Install dependencies during the build CMD Define what runs when the container starts C O M M O N I N S T R U C T I O N S A few more you will use ENV Set environment variables inside the image. EXPOSE Document which port the app listens on. ARG Pass build-time variables to the build. ENTRYPOINT Fix the main command; CMD becomes its args. T H E B U I L D P

ironment variables inside the image. EXPOSE Document which port the app listens on. ARG Pass build-time variables to the build. ENTRYPOINT Fix the main command; CMD becomes its args. T H E B U I L D P R O C E S S From Dockerfile to running app docker build -t myapp . SOURCE Dockerfile + code → BUILD docker build layer by layer → ARTIFACT Image myapp:latest → RUNTIME Container docker run R U N N I N G Y O U R O W N I M A G E Build it, then run it 1Write the Dockerfile 2Build a tagged image 3Run it with a port mapping 4Open it in the browser $ docker build -t myapp . Successfully tagged myapp:latest $ docker run -p 8080:5000 myapp Running on http:./0.0.0.0:5000 R E A L - W O R L D U S E C A S E S Where Docker shines Microservices Package each service independently and scale it on its own. CI/CD Pipelines Build and test in clean, identical environments every time. Local Development Spin up databases and tools without polluting your machine. Cloud Deployment Ship the same image to any clou

/CD Pipelines Build and test in clean, identical environments every time. Local Development Spin up databases and tools without polluting your machine. Cloud Deployment Ship the same image to any cloud that runs containers. D O C K E R I N T H E C L O U D The same image runs everywhere AWS ECS & Fargate EKS (Kubernetes) ECR registry Google Cloud Cloud Run GKE (Kubernetes) Artifact Registry Azure Container Apps AKS (Kubernetes) Container Registry C O M M O N M I S T A K E S Watch out for these Storing data in containers Use volumes, or your data vanishes on removal. Huge images Pick slim base images and clean up build steps. Running as root Create a non-root user for safer containers. Using latest everywhere Pin versions so builds stay reproducible. T H E B I G P I C T U R E How it all connects Docker ImagesContainersDockerfileVolumes Docker Hubbuild Q U I C K C H E C K Test yourself Q1 What is the difference between an image and a container? Q2 Which command publishes a container port

ects Docker ImagesContainersDockerfileVolumes Docker Hubbuild Q U I C K C H E C K Test yourself Q1 What is the difference between an image and a container? Q2 Which command publishes a container port to the host? Q3 Why do we use volumes instead of writing inside a container? K E Y T A K E A W A Y S What to remember Consistency Containers package the app and its dependencies to run anywhere. Images vs Containers An image is the blueprint; a container is a running instance. Dockerfile A repeatable recipe that builds your image step by step. Volumes Keep data safe and persistent beyond the container lifecycle. T H A N K Y O U Questions & Discussion Let’s talk through anything from images and containers to Dockerfiles and deploying to the cloud. IS Imran Sayyed Docker Fundamentals & Containerization
ization

E N G I N E E R I N G E L E C T I V E · C L O U D T R A C K Linux Admin & Networking Process Management, Cron Scheduling & Networking Fundamentals ~2h 30m/Lecture by: Imran Sayyed imran@ubuntu:~$ uptime up 12 days, load 0.14 imran@ubuntu:~$ whoami imran imran@ubuntu:~$ hostname cloud-node-01 S E S S I O N R O A D M A P What we cover today 01 Administration role, servers, duties 02 Processes ps, top, kill, signals 03 Scheduling cron, crontab, jobs 04 Networking IP, DNS, ping, ip addr T H E R O L E What is Linux administration? Keeping Linux servers healthy, secure, and running the services users depend on. Admin manages → Servers configure, secure → Services D A Y T O D A Y Responsibilities of an admin Users accounts, groups, permissions Processes monitor, kill, schedule Storage disks, backups, cleanup Network IPs, DNS, connectivity Security updates, firewall, access Services web, database, uptime P R O G R A M S I N M O T I O N What is a process? Program file on disk → Process running

ups, cleanup Network IPs, DNS, connectivity Security updates, firewall, access Services web, database, uptime P R O G R A M S I N M O T I O N What is a process? Program file on disk → Process running instance → Execution B I R T H T O E X I T Process lifecycle New → Ready → Running → Waiting → Terminated Waiting ↺ Ready while the CPU is busy elsewhere W H O S T A R T E D W H O M Parent and child processes Parent (bash) ↳ forks Child 1Child 2 Every process has a PPID pointing to its parent. The parent waits, then reaps the child when it exits. T H E P R O C E S S T R E E Process hierarchy in Linux systemd PID 1 ↓ sshdnginxcron ↓ bashworkerbackup.sh W H E R E I T R U N S Foreground vs background Foreground Holds the terminal until it finishes. $ ./build.sh Background Runs while you keep using the shell. $ ./build.sh & L O O K I N G I N S I D E Viewing processes ps snapshot of processes top live, refreshing view htop interactive, colourful S N A P S H O T The ps command Lists processes ru

g the shell. $ ./build.sh & L O O K I N G I N S I D E Viewing processes ps snapshot of processes top live, refreshing view htop interactive, colourful S N A P S H O T The ps command Lists processes running right now with their PID and command. terminal imran@ubuntu:~$ ps PID TTY TIME CMD 2417 pts/0 00:00:00 bash 2980 pts/0 00:00:00 ps S E E I N G E V E R Y T H I N G Common ps options aux shows every process for every user. terminal imran@ubuntu:~$ ps aux USER PID %CPU %MEM COMMAND root 1 0.0 0.4 systemd www 1421 2.1 1.8 nginx imran 2417 0.0 0.3 bash L I V E V I E W The top command A real-time dashboard that refreshes every few seconds. top top - 14:22:01 up 12 days, load 0.42 Tasks: 142 total, 1 running %Cpu(s): 7.3 us, 2.1 sy, 90.6 id PID USER %CPU %MEM COMMAND 1421 www 2.1 1.8 nginx 3902 imran 1.4 4.2 node 2417 imran 0.0 0.3 bash R E A D I N G T O P Understanding CPU usage us user programs sy kernel / system id idle, free capacity High us + low id = the CPU is the bottleneck R A M A

imran 1.4 4.2 node 2417 imran 0.0 0.3 bash R E A D I N G T O P Understanding CPU usage us user programs sy kernel / system id idle, free capacity High us + low id = the CPU is the bottleneck R A M A T A G L A N C E Understanding memory usage imran@ubuntu:~$ free -h total used free Mem: 7.6G 3.1G 4.5G Swap: 2.0G 0.0G 2.0G used — held by processes free — available now swap — disk used as overflow F I N D I N G T H E C U L P R I T Spotting heavy processes Sort ps by CPU to find what is hogging the server. terminal imran@ubuntu:~$ ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head USER PID %CPU %MEM COMMAND imran 3902 98.7 4.2 node www 1421 2.1 1.8 nginx S T O P P I N G W O R K The kill command Sends a signal to a process by its PID asking it to stop. terminal imran@ubuntu:~$ kill 3902 [asks PID 3902 to terminate] imran@ubuntu:~$ kill -SIGTERM 3902 [same polite request] M E S S A G E S T O P R O C E S S E S The signal concept kill sends signal → Kernel delivers it → Process SIGTERM asks nicely · SIGKILL forces ·

mran@ubuntu:~$ kill -SIGTERM 3902 [same polite request] M E S S A G E S T O P R O C E S S E S The signal concept kill sends signal → Kernel delivers it → Process SIGTERM asks nicely · SIGKILL forces · SIGHUP reloads T H E L A S T R E S O R T kill -9 Sends SIGKILL. The process cannot ignore it and stops at once. Use only when a process is frozen — it gets no chance to clean up. terminal imran@ubuntu:~$ kill -9 3902 [3902 killed immediately] imran@ubuntu:~$ ps -p 3902 no such process Y O U R T U R N Process management exercise 1 List all processes with ps aux. 2 Find the top CPU user in top. 3 Stop it with kill, then verify. W O R K W H I L E Y O U S L E E P Why task scheduling matters Clock time arrives → Scheduled task triggers → Execution T H E S C H E D U L E R Introduction to cron A background service that runs commands automatically at set times. crond the daemon that checks the clock crontab your list of timed jobs H O W C R O N F I T S T O G E T H E R Crontab architecture User →

kground service that runs commands automatically at set times. crond the daemon that checks the clock crontab your list of timed jobs H O W C R O N F I T S T O G E T H E R Crontab architecture User → Crontab → Cron daemon → Script Edit jobs with crontab -e, list them with crontab -l F I V E F I E L D S Crontab syntax * Minute 0–59 * Hour 0–23 * Day 1–31 * Month 1–12 * Weekday 0–6 30 2 * * * /home/imran/backup.sh R E A D I N G S C H E D U L E S Cron scheduling examples 0 * * * * every hour, on the hour 30 2 * * * every day at 02:30 0 9 * * 1 every Monday at 09:00 -/15 * * * *every 15 minutes O N R E A L S E R V E R S Real world cron use cases Backups copy data every night Cleanup delete old logs and temp files Reports email stats each morning Health checks ping services on a timer E X A M P L E O N E Daily backup Archive a folder to a backup directory every night at 02:00. crontab -e # minute hour day month weekday 0 2 * * * tar -czf \ /backup/site.tgz /var/www E X A M P L E T W O Autom

L E O N E Daily backup Archive a folder to a backup directory every night at 02:00. crontab -e # minute hour day month weekday 0 2 * * * tar -czf \ /backup/site.tgz /var/www E X A M P L E T W O Automated cleanup Delete log files older than seven days, every Sunday at midnight. crontab -e # every Sunday at 00:00 0 0 * * 0 find /var/log \ -name "*.log" -mtime +7 -delete M A C H I N E S T A L K I N G Introduction to networking Device your machine → Router directs traffic → Internet T H E C L O U D I S A N E T W O R K Why networking matters in cloud Access reach servers by IP and DNS Connect apps talk to databases and APIs Debug find why a service is unreachable A D D R E S S E S O N A N E T W O R K What is an IP address? A unique number that identifies a device so data knows where to go. IPv4 192.168.1.10 four numbers, 0–255 IPv6 2001:db8-:1 much larger space T W O K I N D S O F A D D R E S S Public vs private IP Public Reachable from the internet. 203.0.113.5 Private Used inside a local

4 192.168.1.10 four numbers, 0–255 IPv6 2001:db8-:1 much larger space T W O K I N D S O F A D D R E S S Public vs private IP Public Reachable from the internet. 203.0.113.5 Private Used inside a local network only. 192.168.1.10 T A L K I N G T O Y O U R S E L F localhost and 127.0.0.1 Your machine ↺ 127.0.0.1 request loops back, never leaves localhost always points to the machine you are on. Great for testing a web app before it goes live. W H O A M I ? The hostname command Shows the name of the machine you are working on. bash imran@ubuntu:~$ hostname ubuntu-server imran@ubuntu:~$ hostname -I 192.168.1.10 I S I T R E A C H A B L E ? The ping command Sends small packets to test if a host responds, and how fast. bash imran@ubuntu:~$ ping google.com 64 bytes from 142.250.x.x: time=12.4 ms 64 bytes from 142.250.x.x: time=11.8 ms 3 packets transmitted, 3 received, 0% loss T H E I N T E R N E T P H O N E B O O K DNS basics Name google.com → DNS looks up the number → 142.250.x.x E N D T O E

bytes from 142.250.x.x: time=11.8 ms 3 packets transmitted, 3 received, 0% loss T H E I N T E R N E T P H O N E B O O K DNS basics Name google.com → DNS looks up the number → 142.250.x.x E N D T O E N D What happens when you visit a site 1 You type google.com 2 DNS turns the name into an IP address 3 Your request travels to that server 4 The server sends the page back to you I N S P E C T I N T E R F A C E S The ip addr command Lists your network interfaces and the IP addresses assigned to them. bash imran@ubuntu:~$ ip addr 2: eth0: <BROADCAST,UP> inet 192.168.1.10/24 link/ether 08:00:27:ab:cd:ef A S I M P L E C H E C K L I S T Troubleshooting connectivity ping 127.0.0.1 Is my own stack alive? ping gateway Can I reach the router? ping 8.8.8.8 Is the internet reachable? ping google.com Is DNS working too? T R Y I T Y O U R S E L F Networking exercise 1 Find your machine name and IP. hostname -I 2 Ping a public website four times. ping -c 4 google.com 3 List your network interfaces. ip

DNS working too? T R Y I T Y O U R S E L F Networking exercise 1 Find your machine name and IP. hostname -I 2 Ping a public website four times. ping -c 4 google.com 3 List your network interfaces. ip addr S E C T I O N R E C A P Administration summary The role admins keep systems running, secure and backed up Automation cron runs jobs on a schedule, no human needed Mindset prevent problems before they happen S E C T I O N R E C A P Process management summary ps snapshot of running processes top live view of CPU and memory kill send a signal to stop a process cron run jobs automatically on time S E C T I O N R E C A P Networking summary IP address unique number for each device DNS turns names into IP addresses ping test if a host responds ip / hostname inspect your own machine T H E B I G P I C T U R E Revision mind map Linux Server Skills ↓ Administration role · cron · automation Processes ps · top · kill · signals Networking IP · DNS · ping · ip addr C H E C K Y O U R S E L F Quiz rec

I C T U R E Revision mind map Linux Server Skills ↓ Administration role · cron · automation Processes ps · top · kill · signals Networking IP · DNS · ping · ip addr C H E C K Y O U R S E L F Quiz recap Q1 Which command shows a live view of CPU usage? Q2 What does kill -9 do to a process? Q3 What does DNS translate, and into what? Q4 Write a cron line for daily 2:30 AM.

Cloud Computing Course Assignment 1 – Linux Fundamentals & Shell Scripting Prepared by: Imran Sayyed (Course Instructor) Submission Instructions 1. Create a Word or PDF document and answer all theory questions using the corresponding question numbers. 2. For practical questions, include screenshots with the question number clearly mentioned. 3. Ensure screenshots clearly show the command executed and the output. 4. Submit a single PDF containing all answers and screenshots. 5. Total Marks: 50 + 5 Bonus Marks. Section A – Linux Commands (10 Marks) Q1. Create the cloud_course folder structure with notes, assignments and projects folders. Attach screenshot. Q2. Create linux.txt and commands.txt inside notes. Copy linux.txt into projects. Attach screenshot. Q3. Rename commands.txt to linux_commands.txt. Attach screenshot. Q4. Delete the assignments directory. Attach screenshot. Q5. Display the current directory and hidden files. Write the commands used. Section B – Permissions & Ownership

mands.txt. Attach screenshot. Q4. Delete the assignments directory. Attach screenshot. Q5. Display the current directory and hidden files. Write the commands used. Section B – Permissions & Ownership (10 Marks) Q6. Create secret.txt and change permissions to 644. Attach screenshot. Q7. Change permissions to 755 and explain rwxr-xr-x. Q8. Explain the difference between chmod 777 and chmod 644. Q9. Write the purpose of chmod, chown and chgrp. Q10. Explain Owner, Group and Others. Section C – Users & Environment Variables (10 Marks) Q11. Write the purpose of whoami, id and groups. Q12. Display your username and attach screenshot. Q13. Display the HOME environment variable and attach screenshot. Q14. Create a variable NAME with your name and display it. Attach screenshot. Section D – Shell Scripting (10 Marks) Q15. Write a shell script that prints 'Welcome to Cloud Computing'. Q16. Write a shell script that stores your name in a variable and prints it. Q17. Write a shell script that accept

ipting (10 Marks) Q15. Write a shell script that prints 'Welcome to Cloud Computing'. Q16. Write a shell script that stores your name in a variable and prints it. Q17. Write a shell script that accepts user input and displays 'Welcome '. Q18. Write a shell script using an if condition to check whether a number is greater than 10. Q19. Write a for loop that prints numbers from 1 to 5. Section E – Linux Administration (10 Marks) Q20. Display all running processes. Write the command used. Q21. Write the purpose of ps, top and kill. Q22. What is Crontab? Provide one real-world use case. Q23. What is the purpose of ping? Q24. Display your hostname. Write the command used. Q25. Explain the difference between Public IP and Private IP. Bonus Challenge (5 Marks) Write a shell script that creates a folder named student, creates details.txt, writes your name into the file and displays the contents. folder named student, creates details.txt, writes your name into the file and displays the contents. Attach the script and output screenshot.

E N G I N E E R I N G F O U N D A T I O N S · C L O U D T R A C K Cloud Fundamentals Foundations of Modern Cloud Computing ~2h 30m/Lecture by: Imran Sayyed C L O U D S T A C K Applications ↓ Platform ↓ Infrastructure C O U R S E R O A D M A P What we cover today 01 Foundations what cloud is, why it exists 02 Virtualization VMs, hypervisors, containers 03 Service Models IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS 04 Deployment public, private, hybrid A S H O R T H I S T O R Y Evolution of computing 1950s Mainframes shared central machines → 1980s Personal Computers one machine per desk → 2000s Data Centers racks of servers ↓ 2010s+ Cloud Computingon-demand resources over the internet T H E O L D W A Y H U R T S Problems with traditional infrastructure High Cost buy servers up front before any users arrive Maintenance staff to patch, cool, and repair hardware Scaling weeks to add capacity for a traffic spike Downtime one failed machine can stop the business C O R E D E F I N I T I O N What is cloud computing

enance staff to patch, cool, and repair hardware Scaling weeks to add capacity for a traffic spike Downtime one failed machine can stop the business C O R E D E F I N I T I O N What is cloud computing? Cloud computing is the delivery of computing resources servers, storage, networking, and software over the internet, on demand, paying only for what you use. on demandover the internet pay per use Y O U A L R E A D Y U S E I T Cloud computing, explained simply Netflix Movies stream from servers you never see or own. Google Drive Files live online, reachable from any device. Gmail Email runs in a browser, nothing installed locally. T H E C L O U D W H E E L Characteristics of cloud computing On Demand request resources instantly Self Service no human in the loop Elasticity grow and shrink automatically Scalability handle huge load when needed Availability running around the clock Pay As You Go billed only for usage B U I L D I N G B L O C K S What makes up a computer? layer 01 Hardware CP

omatically Scalability handle huge load when needed Availability running around the clock Pay As You Go billed only for usage B U I L D I N G B L O C K S What makes up a computer? layer 01 Hardware CPU, RAM, disk, the physical machine layer 02 Operating System manages and shares the hardware layer 03 Networking connects the machine to others layer 04 Storage keeps data and files persistent T H E B I G S H I F T Why businesses moved to cloud Traditional Buy hardware up front Weeks to add capacity You run the data center Pay even when idle Cloud Rent what you need Scale in minutes Provider runs the hardware Pay only for usage W H Y I T W I N S Cloud benefits Cost no upfront hardware, pay per use Speed launch servers in minutes Scalability grow with demand instantly Global Reach deploy near users worldwide W H E R E C L O U D L I V E S Data center introduction A data center is a building full of servers, storage, cooling, and power that runs the cloud behind the scenes. powercooling netwo

sers worldwide W H E R E C L O U D L I V E S Data center introduction A data center is a building full of servers, storage, cooling, and power that runs the cloud behind the scenes. powercooling network T H E R A W M A C H I N E Physical servers Dedicated one company owns the whole box Powerful strong CPU, large memory and disk Fixed capacity is set by the hardware bought W A S T E D C A P A C I T Y Why physical servers are inefficient T Y P I C A L U S A G E 15% of one server's power is actually used most of the time One app per server most of the box sits idle Always powered full power and cooling cost even when idle Hard to share spare capacity cannot be reused easily O N E B O X , M A N Y M A C H I N E S Introduction to virtualization O N E Physical Server → VM 1 VM 2VM 3 Each virtual machine acts like its own independent computer. T H E S T A C K Virtualization architecture VM 1VM 2VM 3 ↑ Hypervisor splits hardware into virtual machines ↑ Physical Hardware CPU · RAM · storage · ne

machine acts like its own independent computer. T H E S T A C K Virtualization architecture VM 1VM 2VM 3 ↑ Hypervisor splits hardware into virtual machines ↑ Physical Hardware CPU · RAM · storage · network W H Y I T M A T T E R S Benefits of virtualization Better utilization one machine runs many workloads Isolation a crash in one VM never touches the others Fast provisioning spin up a new server in minutes Lower cost fewer physical machines to buy and power T H E M A N A G E R What is a hypervisor? A hypervisor is the software layer that creates and runs virtual machines, sharing one set of hardware among many of them. creates VMsshares hardware keeps them isolated R E S O U R C E A L L O C A T I O N Hypervisor responsibilities Hardware Pool CPU · RAM · disk · network → Allocate give each VM its slice Schedule share CPU time fairly Isolate keep VMs separate Monitor track usage and health B A R E M E T A L Type 1 hypervisor VMs ↑ Hypervisor ↑ Hardware Runs directly on the hardware, wit

VM its slice Schedule share CPU time fairly Isolate keep VMs separate Monitor track usage and health B A R E M E T A L Type 1 hypervisor VMs ↑ Hypervisor ↑ Hardware Runs directly on the hardware, with no host operating system in between. E X A M P L E S ESXi · Hyper-V · KVM H O S T E D Type 2 hypervisor VMs ↑ Hypervisor App ↑ Host OS ↑ Hardware Runs as an app on top of a normal operating system, like any other program. E X A M P L E S VirtualBox · VMware Workstation S I D E B Y S I D E Hypervisor comparison AspectType 1Type 2 Runs on bare hardwarea host OS Performance highmoderate Best for data centerslaptops, testing V I R T U A L O R R E A L Virtual machines vs physical machines Physical Machine One operating system Fixed, dedicated hardware Slow to provision Hard to move or copy Virtual Machine Many on one machine Shares a hardware pool Created in minutes Easy to clone and migrate L I G H T E R T H A N V M S Containers introduction A container packages an app with everything it nee

l Machine Many on one machine Shares a hardware pool Created in minutes Easy to clone and migrate L I G H T E R T H A N V M S Containers introduction A container packages an app with everything it needs and shares the host operating system instead of running a full one. lightweightfast start portable AppAppApp ↑ Container Engine ↑ Host OS + Hardware K E Y C O M P A R I S O N VM vs container Virtual Machine App Guest OS Hypervisor Hardware Each VM ships a full guest OS, so it is heavier and slower to start. Container App Container Engine Shared Host OS Hardware Containers share the host OS, so they are small and start in seconds. N O S E R V E R S T O M A N A G E Introduction to serverless With serverless, you write code and the provider runs it on demand. Servers still exist, but you never see or manage them. No setup no servers to provision Auto scale handles one or millions of calls Pay per run billed only while code runs F R O M H E A V Y T O L I G H T Evolution of compute Physical

see or manage them. No setup no servers to provision Auto scale handles one or millions of calls Pay per run billed only while code runs F R O M H E A V Y T O L I G H T Evolution of compute Physical Machines → Virtual Machines → Containers → Serverless full hardware per appmany VMs per machinemany apps per OSjust your code H O W M U C H Y O U M A N A G E Service models overview IaaS rent the infrastructure PaaS rent the platform SaaS use the software FaaS run just functions you manage more———————————————→provider manages more W H O D O E S W H A T Shared responsibility Provider manages Data centers and hardware Networking and storage Virtualization layer You manage Your apps and data Access and permissions Configuration choices The higher the service model, the more the provider handles for you. I N F R A S T R U C T U R E A S A S E R V I C E IaaS Applications Runtime Operating System Virtual Hardware You rent raw compute, storage, and networking, then build everything on top yourself

or you. I N F R A S T R U C T U R E A S A S E R V I C E IaaS Applications Runtime Operating System Virtual Hardware You rent raw compute, storage, and networking, then build everything on top yourself. E X A M P L E S AWS EC2 · Compute Engine · Azure VM I N P R A C T I C E IaaS example A startup needs servers for a new app but does not want to buy hardware. Rent a VM spin up EC2 in minutes → Install stack OS, runtime, database Scale on demand add servers as traffic grows P L A T F O R M A S A S E R V I C E PaaS Your Code Runtime Operating System Virtual Hardware You deploy your code and the provider runs the platform underneath it for you. E X A M P L E S App Engine · Azure App Service · Heroku I N P R A C T I C E PaaS example A developer wants to ship a web app without managing servers or operating systems. Write code build the app locally → Push to deploy platform builds and runs it Auto managed scaling and patching handled S O F T W A R E A S A S E R V I C E SaaS Ready-to-Use App ↑

operating systems. Write code build the app locally → Push to deploy platform builds and runs it Auto managed scaling and patching handled S O F T W A R E A S A S E R V I C E SaaS Ready-to-Use App ↑ provider runs everything below You just log in and use the software. The provider handles all the layers beneath it. E X A M P L E S Gmail · Office 365 · Zoom I N P R A C T I C E SaaS example A team needs email and video calls without installing or maintaining any software. Open a browser no install needed → Sign in use it instantly Always updated provider patches it for you F U N C T I O N S A S A S E R V I C E FaaS You upload small functions that run only when an event triggers them. Event upload, request, timer → Function runs spins up on demand → Result stop, pay per run examples: AWS Lambda · Cloud Functions · Azure Functions A L L T O G E T H E R Service model comparison ModelYou manageExample IaaS OS, runtime, appsAWS EC2 PaaS just your codeApp Engine SaaS nothing, just use itGmail

da · Cloud Functions · Azure Functions A L L T O G E T H E R Service model comparison ModelYou manageExample IaaS OS, runtime, appsAWS EC2 PaaS just your codeApp Engine SaaS nothing, just use itGmail FaaS single functionsAWS Lambda W H E R E T H E C L O U D L I V E S Deployment models Public shared infrastructure run by a provider Private dedicated to a single organization Hybrid a mix of public and private S H A R E D B Y M A N Y Public cloud Infrastructure owned by a provider and shared across many customers over the internet. No hardware to buy Pay only for what you use Scales almost instantly AWS · Azure · Google Cloud Shared Provider Cloud Company ACompany B Startup CUser D F O R O N E O R G A N I Z A T I O N Private cloud Single Organization Team A Team B Team C Infrastructure dedicated to one organization, for more control and security. Full control over resources Strong privacy and compliance Higher cost to run B E S T O F B O T H Hybrid cloud A mix of private and public cloud,

ted to one organization, for more control and security. Full control over resources Strong privacy and compliance Higher cost to run B E S T O F B O T H Hybrid cloud A mix of private and public cloud, connected so workloads can move between them. Private Cloud sensitive data and core systems ↔ Public Cloud bursts of traffic and scale Keep critical data private while using the public cloud for extra capacity. S I D E B Y S I D E Deployment comparison AspectPublicPrivateHybrid Cost lowhighbalanced Control lowhighflexible Best for startups, web appsbanks, hospitalslarge enterprises Y O U U S E I T D A I L Y Cloud in real life Netflix streams video from the public cloud at global scale Google Photos stores and backs up your photos in the cloud Spotify delivers music on demand from cloud servers Online Banking runs securely on private and hybrid clouds T I E I T T O G E T H E R Revision mind map Cloud Computing ↓ Virtualization VMs, hypervisors, containers Service Models IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, F

s Online Banking runs securely on private and hybrid clouds T I E I T T O G E T H E R Revision mind map Cloud Computing ↓ Virtualization VMs, hypervisors, containers Service Models IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS Deployment public, private, hybrid Benefits scale, cost, speed C H E C K Y O U R S E L F Quick quiz Q1 What does a hypervisor do? Q2 How is a container different from a VM? Q3 Which model is Gmail: IaaS, PaaS, or SaaS? Q4 When would you choose a hybrid cloud? W R A P U P Key takeaways Cloud is on-demand computing resources over the internet, billed by use Virtualization makes it possible VMs and containers share hardware efficiently Service models set the line IaaS to SaaS shift work to the provider Deployment fits the need public, private, or hybrid by priority
